The **Parliament of New South Wales**, formally the **Legislature of New South Wales**,[[6]](#cite_note-:0-8)[[7]](#cite_note-:1-9) is the [bicameral](/wiki/Bicameralism "Bicameralism") legislative body of the [Australian](/wiki/Australia "Australia") [state](/wiki/States_and_territories_of_Australia "States and territories of Australia") of [New South Wales](/wiki/New_South_Wales "New South Wales") (NSW). It consists of the [Monarch](/wiki/Monarchy_of_Australia "Monarchy of Australia"), the [New South Wales Legislative Assembly](/wiki/New_South_Wales_Legislative_Assembly "New South Wales Legislative Assembly") (lower house) and the [New South Wales Legislative Council](/wiki/New_South_Wales_Legislative_Council "New South Wales Legislative Council") (upper house).[[6]](#cite_note-:0-8)[[8]](#cite_note-10) Each house is directly elected by the people of New South Wales at elections held approximately every four years. The legislative authority of the parliament derives from section 5 of the *[Constitution Act 1902](/wiki/Constitution_Act_1902 "Constitution Act 1902")* (NSW).[[9]](#cite_note-11) The power to make laws that apply to New South Wales is shared with the [Federal (or Commonwealth) Parliament](/wiki/Parliament_of_Australia "Parliament of Australia"). The houses of the New South Wales Parliament follow the [Westminster parliamentary traditions](/wiki/Westminster_system "Westminster system"), green and red chamber colours and protocols for the Legislative Assembly and the Legislative Council, respectively.[[10]](#cite_note-NSW-12) The houses of the legislature are located in [Parliament House](/wiki/Parliament_House,_Sydney "Parliament House, Sydney") on [Macquarie Street](/wiki/Macquarie_Street,_Sydney "Macquarie Street, Sydney") in [Sydney](/wiki/Sydney "Sydney").

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Parliament_of_New_South_Wales&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

The Parliament of New South Wales was the first of the Australian colonial legislatures, with its formation in the 1850s. At the time, New South Wales was a [British colony](/wiki/Colony_of_New_South_Wales "Colony of New South Wales") under the control of the [Governor](/wiki/Governor_of_New_South_Wales "Governor of New South Wales"). A small, appointed Legislative Council began meeting in 1824 to advise the Governor on legislative matters. By 1843, this had been enlarged, with two-thirds of its members elected by adult males who met certain property requirements.[[11]](#cite_note-LA-13)

In 1850, the *[Australian Colonies Government Act](/wiki/Australian_Constitutions_Act_1850 "Australian Constitutions Act 1850")* was passed by the [Imperial Parliament](/wiki/Parliament_of_the_United_Kingdom "Parliament of the United Kingdom"), which expanded the New South Wales Legislative Council so that by 1851 there were 54 members – again, with two-thirds elected. In 1853, a select committee chaired by [William Charles Wentworth](/wiki/William_Wentworth "William Wentworth") began drawing up a constitution for responsible self-government in the colony. While many of the provisions in the committee's proposed constitution were accepted when placed before the Legislative Council in August that year, a [proposal to create a colonial nobility](/wiki/Bunyip_aristocracy "Bunyip aristocracy") was subject to heavy criticism and later withdrawn.[[12]](#cite_note-14)

The approved [bicameral](/wiki/Bicameralism "Bicameralism") structure included a fully elected Legislative Assembly, as well as a Legislative Council whose members were appointed for life. A [government](/wiki/Government_of_New_South_Wales "Government of New South Wales") assumed most of the Governor's legislative powers. The new constitution was sent to the Imperial Parliament and passed into law on 16 July 1855. On 22 May 1856, the newly constituted New South Wales Parliament opened and sat for the first time. With the new 54-member Legislative Assembly taking over the Council chamber, a second meeting chamber for the 21-member upper house had to be added to the Parliament building on Macquarie Street.[[13]](#cite_note-15)

The *Electoral Act of 1858* made additional changes.[[14]](#cite_note-16) The right to vote was extended to most males over 21 years of age.[[11]](#cite_note-LA-13) However, men "in the receipt of aid from any charitable institution" were ineligible. This was deemed to include all people living on [Aboriginal stations and reserves](/wiki/Aboriginal_reserve "Aboriginal reserve").[[15]](#cite_note-17) Other men not able to vote included those "of unsound mind", incarcerated individuals, and members of the military or the police.

In 1859 [Queensland](/wiki/Queensland "Queensland") separated from New South Wales to form an independent colony. The Legislative Assembly was reduced from 80 to 72 members by the loss of the Queensland seats.[[11]](#cite_note-LA-13) In 1901, New South Wales became a state of the [Commonwealth of Australia](/wiki/Commonwealth_of_Australia "Commonwealth of Australia"), and many government functions were transferred to the new Commonwealth government. In 1902, the current [constitution of New South Wales](/wiki/Constitution_Act_1902 "Constitution Act 1902") was adopted.[[16]](#cite_note-18)

Women [gained the right to vote](/wiki/Women%27s_suffrage_in_Australia "Women's suffrage in Australia") in Commonwealth elections in April 1902, and in New South Wales state elections in August 1902.[[11]](#cite_note-LA-13)[[17]](#cite_note-19) In 1918, reforms permitted women to be members of Parliament, although no woman was elected until 1925 when [Millicent Preston-Stanley](/wiki/Millicent_Preston-Stanley "Millicent Preston-Stanley") was elected to represent the [Eastern Suburbs](/wiki/Electoral_district_of_Eastern_Suburbs_(New_South_Wales) "Electoral district of Eastern Suburbs (New South Wales)"). That same year, a [proportional representation](/wiki/Proportional_representation "Proportional representation") system was introduced for the Legislative Assembly with multiple representatives from each electorate; this system lasted until it was abolished in 1926.[[11]](#cite_note-LA-13) Women were not able to be appointed to the Legislative Council until 1926; Premier [John Storey](/wiki/John_Storey_(politician) "John Storey (politician)") attempted to appoint [Kate Dwyer](/wiki/Kate_Dwyer "Kate Dwyer") to the Legislative Council in 1921, but the appointment was ruled out of order.[[18]](#cite_note-women-20) The first two women appointed to the Legislative Council were both [Labor Party](/wiki/New_South_Wales_Labor_Party "New South Wales Labor Party") members proposed on 23 November 1931: [Catherine Green](/wiki/Catherine_Green "Catherine Green"), who took her seat the following day, and [Ellen Webster](/wiki/Ellen_Webster "Ellen Webster"), who joined her two days later.[[18]](#cite_note-women-20)

In 1925, 1926, and 1929, Premier [Jack Lang](/wiki/Jack_Lang_(Australian_politician) "Jack Lang (Australian politician)") made unsuccessful attempts to abolish the Legislative Council, following the example of the [Queensland Legislative Council](/wiki/Queensland_Legislative_Council "Queensland Legislative Council") in 1922. These attempts led to further reform, and in 1933 the law was changed so that a quarter of the Legislative Council would be elected every three years by members of the Legislative Assembly and the Legislative Council, rather than being appointed by the Governor.

From 1926, people receiving aid could again be on the electoral roll. [Compulsory voting](/wiki/Compulsory_voting_in_Australia "Compulsory voting in Australia") was introduced in 1928.[[19]](#cite_note-21)

In 1978, the Council became a directly elected body in a program of electoral reform introduced by the [Wran](/wiki/Neville_Wran "Neville Wran") Labor government. The number of members was reduced to 45, although transitional arrangements meant that there were 43 members from 1978 to 1981, and 44 from 1981 to 1984. Further reform in 1991 by the [Greiner](/wiki/Nick_Greiner "Nick Greiner") [Liberal](/wiki/New_South_Wales_Liberal_Party "New South Wales Liberal Party")–[National](/wiki/New_South_Wales_National_Party "New South Wales National Party") government saw the size of the Legislative Council cut to 42 members, with half being elected every 4 years. In [1991](/wiki/1991_New_South_Wales_state_election "1991 New South Wales state election"), the Legislative Assembly was reduced from 109 to 99 members and then to 93 members in [1999](/wiki/1999_New_South_Wales_state_election "1999 New South Wales state election").[[11]](#cite_note-LA-13)

### Parliament House

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Parliament_of_New_South_Wales&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Parliament House")]

Main article: [Parliament House, Sydney](/wiki/Parliament_House,_Sydney "Parliament House, Sydney")

The Parliament building was originally built on the orders of Governor [Lachlan Macquarie](/wiki/Lachlan_Macquarie "Lachlan Macquarie"), to be Sydney's second major hospital. In 1810, he awarded the contract to [Garnham Blaxcell](/wiki/Garnham_Blaxcell "Garnham Blaxcell"), [Alexander Riley](/wiki/Alexander_Riley_(merchant) "Alexander Riley (merchant)") and [D'Arcy Wentworth](/wiki/D%27Arcy_Wentworth "D'Arcy Wentworth"). The contract gave the builders the right to import 45,000 gallons of rum, for which they paid a duty of 3 shillings a gallon. They were able to sell it for a huge profit and in turn the government refunded them the duty as a payment for their work, thereby gaining for their construction the title of the 'Rum Hospital'. Originally consisting of three buildings, the central main building was demolished in 1879 to make way for the new [Sydney Hospital](/wiki/Sydney_Hospital "Sydney Hospital"), which was completed in 1885. The first building, now known as the [Sydney Mint](/wiki/Sydney_Mint "Sydney Mint"), was given to the [Royal Mint](/wiki/Royal_Mint "Royal Mint") in 1851 to become the Australian branch of its operations; it remained a mint until 1927.[[20]](#cite_note-book-22)

The second building, originally built as the Chief Surgeon's quarters, was given to the government in 1829 for the purposes of a Parliament chamber. This chamber was added to following the growth of the legislature in 1843, and again in 1856. The last major renovation to the building was undertaken between 1974 and 1985, which saw a jumble of buildings that had become the parliamentary chambers demolished and replaced by a 12-story block linked by a fountain court to the original Parliament House. The building was also restored to its 1908 appearance.[[20]](#cite_note-book-22)

## Composition and powers

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Parliament_of_New_South_Wales&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Composition and powers")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bb/SLNSW_479505_2_Legislative_Assembly_Chamber_Interior_SH_552.jpg/250px-SLNSW_479505_2_Legislative_Assembly_Chamber_Interior_SH_552.jpg)](/wiki/File:SLNSW_479505_2_Legislative_Assembly_Chamber_Interior_SH_552.jpg)

The [Legislative Assembly](/wiki/New_South_Wales_Legislative_Assembly "New South Wales Legislative Assembly") chamber, with a portrait of [William Charles Wentworth](/wiki/William_Wentworth "William Wentworth") on the left

The legislative authority, the [King-in-Parliament](/wiki/King-in-Parliament "King-in-Parliament"), has three separate elements: the monarch; the Legislative Assembly; and the Legislative Council.[[6]](#cite_note-:0-8)[[7]](#cite_note-:1-9)[[21]](#cite_note-23) This authority is granted by section 5 of the *[Constitution Act 1902](/wiki/Constitution_Act_1902 "Constitution Act 1902")*; an Act that itself was passed by the NSW Parliament under the authority granted to it from the *Constitution Act 1855.* This was an Act originating from a bill from the pre-responsible government Legislative Council, that was slightly modified and given the force of law by an Act of the UK Parliament.[[22]](#cite_note-1855Constitution-24)[[23]](#cite_note-25)

All 93 members of the Legislative Assembly are elected at each general election from [single-member districts](/wiki/Single-member_districts "Single-member districts") using [optional preferential voting](/wiki/Instant-runoff_voting "Instant-runoff voting") to terms of up to four years. The 42 Legislative Council members are elected for two terms (a maximum of eight years), with half elected at each general election. Elections for the Legislative Council are conducted on a statewide, at-large basis (meaning all members represent the entire state) using the [single transferable vote](/wiki/Single_transferable_vote "Single transferable vote") system similar to that used for elections to the [federal Senate](/wiki/Australian_Senate "Australian Senate").

In the running of Parliament, the two presiding officers have a role that is similar to Ministers and their departments. The [Speaker of the Legislative Assembly](/wiki/Speaker_of_the_New_South_Wales_Legislative_Assembly "Speaker of the New South Wales Legislative Assembly") and the [President of the Legislative Council](/wiki/President_of_the_New_South_Wales_Legislative_Council "President of the New South Wales Legislative Council") are responsible for the employment of staff. In consultation with the parliamentary clerks, the presiding officers determine policy for the operation of their respective chambers and jointly for the Parliament.

The final step for a bill passed by the houses of parliament to become a law is the granting of [royal assent](/wiki/Royal_assent "Royal assent") by the Governor on behalf of the King.[[24]](#cite_note-26) Other powers of the governor in relation to Parliament include the power to prorogue,[[25]](#cite_note-27) summon[[26]](#cite_note-28) and dissolve[[27]](#cite_note-29) the houses, issue writs for elections[[28]](#cite_note-30) and administer oaths to new members of parliament.[[29]](#cite_note-31) These powers are in almost all circumstances exercised on the advice of the [premier of New South Wales](/wiki/Premier_of_New_South_Wales "Premier of New South Wales"). The premier and government are directly accountable to Parliament through its control of public finances and the need for its confidence, and to the public through members of Parliament.

The governor chooses the premier, usually depending on the results of the general election, who then forms a government from members of the houses of Parliament. This must be someone who can command the confidence of a majority of members in the Legislative Assembly — usually as part of a political party. This is usually a straightforward decision, though occasionally the governor has to make a judgment, as in August 1939 when the governor, [Lord Wakehurst](/wiki/John_Loder,_2nd_Baron_Wakehurst "John Loder, 2nd Baron Wakehurst"), handled a major political crisis brought about when the former deputy leader of the governing [United Australia Party](/wiki/United_Australia_Party "United Australia Party"), [Eric Spooner](/wiki/Eric_Spooner "Eric Spooner"), brought down Premier [Bertram Stevens](/wiki/Bertram_Stevens_(politician) "Bertram Stevens (politician)") in a [motion of no confidence](/wiki/Motion_of_no_confidence "Motion of no confidence"). Wakehurst asked the treasurer, [Alexander Mair](/wiki/Alexander_Mair "Alexander Mair"), to form a government.[[30]](#cite_note-book2-32)

The current premier of New South Wales is [Chris Minns](/wiki/Chris_Minns "Chris Minns") of the [Labor Party](/wiki/New_South_Wales_Labor_Party "New South Wales Labor Party").

Government ministers (including the premier) must regularly answer questions in the chambers and there are a number of [select committees](/wiki/Select_committee_(parliamentary_system) "Select committee (parliamentary system)") that scrutinise particular issues and the workings of the government. There are also mechanisms that allow members of Parliament to bring to the attention of the government particular issues affecting their constituents.[[31]](#cite_note-33)[[32]](#cite_note-34)

For a bill to become law, it must be passed by both the Legislative Council and the Legislative Assembly and be assented to by the governor. Under Section 5A of the Constitution Act 1902, a bill appropriating revenue for the ordinary annual services of the government can be presented to the governor for assent even if the upper house has not agreed to it.[[33]](#cite_note-35)

## State opening and traditions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Parliament_of_New_South_Wales&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: State opening and traditions")]

Main article: [State Opening of Parliament](/wiki/State_Opening_of_Parliament "State Opening of Parliament")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/3/38/Queen_opening.png/250px-Queen_opening.png)](/wiki/File:Queen_opening.png)

[Queen Elizabeth II](/wiki/Elizabeth_II "Elizabeth II") opening the NSW Parliament on 4 February 1954

The state opening of Parliament is an annual event that marks the commencement of a session of the Parliament of New South Wales. It is held in the Legislative Council Chamber, usually in November or December, or in a [general election](/wiki/General_election "General election") year, when the new Parliament first assembles. It is an occasion for much pomp and ceremony, usually with a guard of honour and with dignitaries of the state attending. The New South Wales Parliament maintains many of the traditions of the original [Parliament of the United Kingdom](/wiki/Parliament_of_the_United_Kingdom "Parliament of the United Kingdom"), from which the New South Wales Parliament was founded.

The Governor, or occasionally the [Monarch](/wiki/Monarch "Monarch"), reads a prepared speech, known as the [Speech from the Throne](/wiki/Speech_from_the_Throne "Speech from the Throne"), outlining the government's agenda for the coming year. The speech is not written by the governor, but rather by the [Cabinet](/wiki/Cabinet_(government) "Cabinet (government)"), and reflects the legislative agenda for which they seek the agreement of both houses of Parliament.

[Queen Elizabeth II](/wiki/Elizabeth_II "Elizabeth II") opened the New South Wales Parliament on two occasions, on 4 February 1954, as part of her [first visit](/wiki/Royal_visits_to_Australia#The_Queen's_first_visit "Royal visits to Australia") to Australia, which was also the first occasion in which a Monarch of Australia had opened a session of any Australian parliament. The other occasion was on 20 February 1992, during her visit to Sydney to celebrate the sesquicentenary of the incorporation of the [City of Sydney](/wiki/City_of_Sydney "City of Sydney"), on which occasion she stated:

> This is my second opportunity to address this Parliament – a Parliament which I described on the previous occasion, in 1954, as the Mother Parliament of Australia. It is interesting to reflect that that was the first time on which the Sovereign had opened a Session of an Australian Parliament. I was also on my first visit to Australia as your Queen. I have returned to New South Wales eight times since then and am always delighted by the warm and generous hospitality accorded to Prince Philip and me by the people of this State. On this occasion I have come to join in commemorating Sydney's first one hundred and fifty years as a city.[[34]](#cite_note-Queen-36)

## Emblem

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Parliament_of_New_South_Wales&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Emblem")]

The official emblem of the New South Wales Parliament is a [crowned](/wiki/St_Edward%27s_Crown "St Edward's Crown") circlet featuring the [coat of arms of New South Wales](/wiki/Coat_of_arms_of_New_South_Wales "Coat of arms of New South Wales") taking the form of a [Scottish crest badge](/wiki/Scottish_crest_badge "Scottish crest badge"). Crest badges, much like [clan tartans](/wiki/Tartans#Clan_tartans "Tartans"), do not have a long history, and owe much to Victorian era romanticism, having only been worn on the bonnet since the mid-19th century when the buckled strap device commonly used by the [Order of the Garter](/wiki/Order_of_the_Garter "Order of the Garter") was adopted as a popular design to encircle monogram escutcheons and heraldic crests.[[35]](#cite_note-Campbell-289–290-37)

The crest badge came to be accepted in the mid-20th century as the emblem of both houses of Parliament. The emblem appears on official stationery, publications and papers, and is stamped on various items in use in the Parliament, such as cutlery, silverware and china.
